text: What is stored inside a Splunk Enterprise index bucket?
explanation: A bucket contains compressed raw data (.gz), a tsidx (time-series index) for fast field lookup, and a Bloom filter for quick term-presence checks. There is no SQL, BKD tree, or KV Store involved.

options.text: KV Store, index files and NoSQL database files
Bloom filter, tsidx and compressed raw data
Inverted indices and BKD trees
B-tree, data pages and SQL indices

text: A Bloom filter tells you that a search term…
explanation: A Bloom filter is a probabilistic structure. A negative answer is definitive — the term is definitely absent. A positive answer means the term MAY be present, so the tsidx still needs checking.

options.text: Definitely exists in the bucket
Definitely does not exist in the bucket
Might exist — or definitely does not exist — in the bucket
Is an indexed field with high cardinality

text: What is the correct order of the Splunk bucket lifecycle?
explanation: Buckets move Hot → Warm → Cold → Frozen as they age. Hot buckets are open for writes; warm and cold are read-only; frozen is archived/deleted.

options.text: Hot → Cold → Warm → Frozen
Warm → Hot → Cold → Frozen
Hot → Warm → Cold → Frozen
Hot → Warm → Frozen → Cold

text: In SmartStore, the cache manager on an indexer mainly…
explanation: The cache manager fetches warm/cold buckets from the remote object store into local cache on demand, and evicts them by policy when space is needed. Hot buckets are still written locally.

options.text: Fetches buckets from remote object storage into local cache and evicts by policy
Runs all user searches so the search head stays idle
Replaces the license manager
Only moves frozen archives to object storage

text: In SmartStore, hot buckets are stored remotely in object storage from the start
explanation: No — hot buckets are still written locally. Only when a bucket rolls from hot to warm does SmartStore upload it to the remote store (e.g. S3). This is the key architectural difference from classic Splunk.

options.text: Yes
No

text: Which components are part of a distributed Splunk Enterprise deployment? (select all that apply)
explanation: A distributed deployment typically includes Universal Forwarders (collect data), Indexers (store and index), Search Heads (run searches), and a Deployment Server (manage UF configs). The License Manager handles licensing.

options.text: Universal Forwarder
Indexer
Search Head
Deployment Server

text: The Splunk data pipeline processes data in which order?
explanation: Data flows: Parsing Queue → Parsing Pipeline (line breaking, charset, timestamps) → Index Queue → Index Pipeline (write to disk). Queues buffer events between pipeline stages.

options.text: Index Pipeline → Parsing Pipeline → Index Queue → Parsing Queue
Parsing Queue → Index Queue → Parsing Pipeline → Index Pipeline
Parsing Queue → Parsing Pipeline → Index Queue → Index Pipeline
Index Queue → Parsing Queue → Index Pipeline → Parsing Pipeline

text: Streaming commands run on indexers in a distributed deployment
explanation: Yes — streaming commands (like eval, where, regex) can run on indexers in parallel before results are sent to the search head. This is why placing them early in the pipeline improves performance.

options.text: Yes
No

text: Which search mode skips non-essential field discovery for the fastest possible results?
explanation: Fast mode skips field discovery and event data unless specifically needed. Smart mode adjusts automatically based on the command used. Verbose mode returns everything.

options.text: Verbose mode
Smart mode
Night mode
Fast mode

text: In Verbose mode, Splunk returns all fields and tags for every event
explanation: Yes — Verbose mode extracts all fields, tags, and event types for every result. It gives the most complete view but is the slowest of the three modes.

options.text: Yes
No

text: The TERM() directive can match terms that span across major breakers like whitespace
explanation: No — TERM() only works within a single segment (between major breakers like space, comma, etc.). If your term contains whitespace, TERM() won't match it. Use quotes instead.

options.text: Yes
No

text: Using a wildcard at the START of a search term (e.g. *error) is…
explanation: Leading wildcards force Splunk to scan every term in every event — the Bloom filter and tsidx cannot help at all. This is one of the most expensive things you can do in a search.

options.text: Fine — wildcards are fully optimised in all positions
Slightly slower than a trailing wildcard
A major performance killer — bypasses all index optimisations
Only a problem on Splunk Cloud

text: Which of these are the 'four horsemen' of bad Splunk searches? (select all that apply)
explanation: All four are classic performance anti-patterns: searching all indexes wastes I/O; All Time scans everything; leading/mid wildcards bypass the tsidx; JOIN is expensive and serial.

options.text: index=* or no index filter at all
All Time as the search time range
Wildcards at the start or middle of a search term
Using JOIN to combine data sources

text: Your best tool for diagnosing exactly where a search spends its time is…
explanation: The Job Inspector breaks down search execution into phases (command.search, command.eval, etc.) with timing, giving you precise insight into bottlenecks.

options.text: Monitoring Console
Job Inspector
SPL Profiler
The search.log file

text: Which of the following are valid search optimisation tips? (select all that apply)
explanation: Always specify the index, restrict time range, and place streaming (filtering) commands early. Wildcards as prefixes are an anti-pattern — they prevent index optimisation.

options.text: Always specify the index you need
Restrict the time range as much as possible
Use wildcards as prefixes for flexible matching
Place streaming commands early in the pipeline

text: tstats is best used at the END of a search pipeline, after streaming commands
explanation: No — tstats is a generating command and must be FIRST (or only) in the pipeline. It reads directly from tsidx files, bypassing raw event scanning entirely.

options.text: Yes
No — tstats is a generating command and must be FIRST

text: Which search is faster and why?
explanation: tstats reads directly from tsidx index files without touching raw events at all. The standard SPL pipeline must read and decompress raw event data before evaluating the stats function.

options.text: index=s4c_weather | stats avg(main.temp) by name
| tstats avg(main.temp) WHERE index=s4c_weather by name
They are exactly the same speed
The first — tstats only works on data models

text: PREFIX() in tstats allows you to…
explanation: PREFIX() lets tstats treat indexed terms that look like key=value pairs as if they were indexed fields — without the overhead of a data model or field extraction at search time.

options.text: Add a custom prefix to every event field name
Treat indexed key=value terms as searchable fields without a data model
Filter searches to a specific index name prefix
Pre-sort events before aggregation

text: A generating command is special because it…
explanation: Generating commands (like tstats, inputlookup, makeresults) produce their own result set and do not need events piped into them. They must appear first in the pipeline (or after a pipe when chained).

options.text: Runs only on the search head captain in a cluster
Must always be immediately followed by stats
Cannot run in distributed search environments
Starts a new result set and does not require events piped into it

text: In Splunk Metrics, dimensions are…
explanation: Dimensions are key-value metadata pairs stored alongside metric measurements (e.g. host=web01, region=eu-west). Metric values themselves are numeric measurements under dot-separated names.

options.text: Numeric measurement values at a point in time
Dot-separated segments of a metric name
Key-value pairs that add contextual metadata to a measurement
Index configuration parameters in indexes.conf

text: mstats works on standard event indexes just like stats
explanation: No — mstats only works on metric indexes (type=metric in indexes.conf). For event indexes you use the regular stats or timechart commands.

options.text: Yes
No

text: Which SPL command converts search results into metric data points and writes them to a metric index?
explanation: mcollect converts events from a search into metric data points and writes them to a metric index. meventcollect is a streaming variant; mstats reads metrics; mcatalog lists available metric names.

options.text: mstats
mcatalog
mcollect
meventconvert

text: What does mcollect do?
explanation: mcollect converts SPL search results into metric data points and writes them to a metric index — bridging the world of events and structured time-series metrics.

options.text: converts events to metrics
convert events to metrics
writes events to a metric index
log to metrics

text: What is a Base Search in a Classic XML dashboard?
explanation: A base search runs once and its results are shared among multiple panels using post-process searches. This avoids running the same expensive SPL multiple times for different visualisations.

options.text: The very first search Splunk runs when it starts up
A shared search whose results are reused by multiple panels via post-processing
A search restricted to the main index only
The default search saved with a dashboard

text: Annotations in Splunk XML dashboards allow you to…
explanation: Annotations overlay event markers (vertical lines or labels) on a time chart from a separate search — useful for correlating incidents, deployments, or alerts with metric trends.

options.text: Add PDF export comments to a dashboard
Overlay event markers from a second search on a time chart
Pin sticky notes to a panel for other users
Highlight fields in the raw event viewer

text: Which of the following are features available in Dashboard Studio but NOT in Classic XML dashboards? (select all that apply)
explanation: Dashboard Studio adds absolute/free-form layout positioning, a visual canvas with full CSS styling, and the Chain Search (post-processing equivalent). Classic XML only supports grid layout.

options.text: Absolute (free-form) panel positioning on a canvas
Chain searches (post-processing equivalent)
Trellis layout for small multiples
Saved searches as a data source

text: Dashboard Studio source code is written in…
explanation: Dashboard Studio uses JSON as its source format, unlike Classic XML dashboards which use SimpleXML. You can edit Studio dashboards directly in the JSON editor.

options.text: XML
YAML
Estonian
JSON

text: A Chain Search in Dashboard Studio is…
explanation: Chain Search is Dashboard Studio's equivalent of post-processing: it takes the results of a base (primary) data source and applies additional SPL without re-running the original search.

options.text: A very slow search that triggers a chain reaction
The Dashboard Studio equivalent of a post-processing search
A search that spans multiple supply-chain data sources
An AI-powered search that combines multiple engines

text: Images uploaded only through the Dashboard Studio UI (stored in KV Store) are a problem when sharing an app because…
explanation: KV Store images are instance-specific and are not included when you export or install an app package. Best practice: bundle images under appserver/static/ and reference them with $env:app_root$.

options.text: They auto-sync to your Git repository and cause merge conflicts
They may not be included when the app is packaged or installed elsewhere
They always live as files under etc/apps and take too much disk space
They delete the dashboard JSON on export for security reasons

text: Splunk Search Assistant in the search bar helps you with… (select all that apply)
explanation: The Search Assistant provides command auto-completion, syntax highlighting, quick field suggestions, and command help — all inline as you type. It does not run searches on your behalf.

options.text: SPL command auto-completion
Inline syntax highlighting and error hints
Field value suggestions based on indexed data
Automatically running and scheduling searches for you

text:
In a Splunk search, inline comments using `

` backtick-triple syntax allow you to…
explanation: Inline comments (using the ``` `comment(...)` ``` macro) let you annotate SPL pipelines without affecting search execution — great for readability and documentation inside a search.

options.text: Disable parts of a search during debugging
Annotate SPL pipelines without affecting search execution
Add notes visible only to the dashboard owner
Insert HTML comments into dashboard panels

text: Drilldown in a Classic XML dashboard can be used to… (select all that apply)
explanation: Drilldown supports navigation to another dashboard, a search, a custom URL, or a field-value based filter. It fires on a user click event inside a panel (chart bar, table row, map region, etc.).

options.text: Navigate to another Splunk dashboard
Run a new search filtered by the clicked value
Open an external URL in the browser
Automatically email the clicked result to a user

text: In Classic XML dashboards, tokens allow you to…
explanation: Tokens store dynamic values (e.g. from drilldown, inputs, or set/unset actions) and can be referenced inside SPL searches, panel titles, and labels using the $token_name$ syntax to create interactive dashboards.

options.text: Password-protect individual panels
Pass dynamic values between panels, searches, and labels at runtime
Replace saved search names with friendly titles
Grant access to specific users only

text: Transforming (non-streaming) commands such as stats and timechart run exclusively on…
explanation: Non-streaming (transforming) commands like stats, timechart, sort, and top must run on the search head because they need all results gathered before producing output. Streaming commands like eval and where can run on indexers.

options.text: All indexers in parallel
The search head, after all indexers return their partial results
The deployment server
Wherever the licence manager resides

text: The Phyphox app used in the workshop lets you…
explanation: Phyphox streams real smartphone sensor data (accelerometer, gyroscope, barometer, etc.) over HTTP in JSON format. In the workshop this is used as a live data source to build searches and dashboards against real IoT-style data.

options.text: Configure Splunk forwarder settings on an Android device
Stream live smartphone sensor data (accelerometer, GPS, etc.) over HTTP as JSON
Run Splunk searches directly from a smartphone
Simulate log data for testing Splunk dashboards

text: Which command lists all metric names and dimensions available in a metric index?
explanation: mcatalog searches metric indexes and returns a catalogue of available metric names (and optionally their dimensions). It's the discovery command for the metrics ecosystem — equivalent to a metadata search for event indexes.

options.text: mstats
mcollect
mcatalog
mpreview

text: Data sources in Dashboard Studio can include… (select all that apply)
explanation: Dashboard Studio supports inline SPL searches, saved searches (referenced by name), and chain searches (post-processing of another data source). Static data sources (hard-coded JSON) are also supported.

options.text: Inline SPL searches
Saved searches referenced by name
Chain searches (post-processing of another source)
Live SQL database queries

text: Splunk Job Settings let you control… (select all that apply)
explanation: Job Settings expose per-search controls: how long cached results are kept (TTL), the time range dispatched, whether real-time search is used, and sampling rate for performance testing.

options.text: How long search results are cached (auto-cancel / TTL)
The time range dispatched to indexers
Event sampling rate for performance testing
Which specific indexers receive the search

text: The Analytics Workspace in Splunk is best suited for working with…
explanation: The Analytics Workspace provides a visual, no-SPL interface optimised for metric indexes — you pick dimensions, measure names, and time ranges graphically. It does not work well with standard event data.

options.text: Standard event indexes using complex SPL pipelines
Metric indexes using a visual, no-SPL interface
KV Store collections via REST API
Machine Learning Toolkit experiments

text: The highlight_terms feature in Splunk search results…
explanation: highlight_terms visually marks the search keywords inside each raw event in the results list, making it easy to spot where matches occurred without reading every character of the event.

options.text: Adds colour to field names in the field sidebar
Highlights your search keywords inside each matching raw event
Marks anomalous events with a warning icon
Bolds the _time field in the results table

text: Rate your overall confidence with the topics covered in this Splunk4Champions workshop
explanation: No wrong answers — just a pulse check to help the trainer calibrate follow-up sessions.

text: Which Splunk topic from this workshop do you want to explore more?
explanation: Word cloud results help the trainer prioritise follow-up content and labs.
